answers.value: Public Works Department – Street Maintenance Division
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Michael J. Anderson, Public Works Director
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Public Works Department – Street Maintenance Division is requesting authorization from the Brainerd City Council to purchase one (1) replacement tandem-axle snowplow truck to replace Unit #23, a 2007 International 7600 plow truck that has reached the end of its useful service life.

Unit #23 has logged approximately 189,000 miles and 11,400 engine hours, almost all of which are from heavy-duty use in winter maintenance operations (plowing, sanding, and salting). Over the last three winter seasons, the truck has experienced an increasing frequency of major mechanical issues, including recurring hydraulic system leaks, corrosion-related failure of the dump body and undercarriage components, and intermittent electrical issues affecting the plow controls and lighting systems. Several repairs have required extended downtime, which has reduced our available fleet during snow events and placed additional strain on the remaining trucks and operators.

In addition to reliability concerns, the current truck does not meet newer safety and efficiency standards. The cab configuration and lighting are outdated compared to our newer units, visibility is reduced in low-light conditions, and the existing spreader controls do not support the more precise application rates that our current snow and ice control policies recommend. We have also been advised by our fleet mechanic and our contracted heavy-equipment vendor that the frame corrosion on Unit #23 will likely require significant structural work if we intend to keep the truck in service beyond another winter or two. The projected cost of those repairs, combined with the risk of additional failures, makes continued investment in this particular vehicle impractical.

Staff have obtained three written quotes through the state cooperative purchasing program for a new tandem-axle plow truck equipped with a front plow, wing plow, underbody scraper, stainless steel v-box spreader, pre-wet system, and updated electronic controls. The recommended vendor is Northland Truck & Equipment of Duluth, Minnesota, which provided the lowest responsive quote while meeting all of the City’s specifications. The proposed truck is a 2027 model-year Freightliner 114SD chassis with a Henderson plow and spreader package. The estimated build and delivery time is 10–12 months from the date of order, which means we must place the order now in order to have the truck available for the 2027–2028 winter season.

This purchase is included in the City’s adopted 2026–2030 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) under Public Works – Street Maintenance Fleet Replacement. The replacement is consistent with our fleet management policy, which targets a 15–18 year replacement cycle for primary plow units depending on condition, mileage, and maintenance history. Unit #23 is currently in its 19th winter season and is the oldest front-line plow in our fleet. Once the new truck is delivered and placed in service, staff intend to auction Unit #23 through the state surplus program, with any sale proceeds being returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Alternatives Options Effects On Others Comments
answers.value:
Alternatives and Options Considered:

1. Defer Replacement for 1–2 Years:
   Staff considered deferring the replacement of Unit #23 for an additional winter or two in order to stretch the life of the existing asset. This option would avoid the immediate capital expenditure in 2026 but would likely result in higher operating and maintenance costs over the short term. Our fleet mechanic estimates that, at a minimum, we would need to invest $18,000–$25,000 in frame repairs, hydraulic line replacements, and electrical system troubleshooting to safely operate Unit #23 for two more winters. Even with that investment, there is a significant risk that additional components (e.g., transmission, rear differential, or spreader drive system) could fail without much warning, given the age and usage profile of the truck.

   Deferring replacement would also mean that our plow fleet remains one truck short of our desired reliability standard. We currently operate eight primary plow routes during major snow events. If even one truck is down, we either have to split routes, extend driver shifts, or delay plowing in some residential areas. During the January 2025 back-to-back storms, Unit #23 was out of service for 3 of 5 events, and response times in the northeast quadrant of the city slipped outside our adopted snow and ice control policy guidelines. We received multiple resident complaints about delayed plowing on side streets and cul-de-sacs, and our staff logged more than 25 hours of overtime to compensate for the missing unit.

2. Purchase a Used or Refurbished Plow Truck:
   Staff also explored the possibility of purchasing a used or refurbished plow truck from neighboring jurisdictions or from commercial dealers who specialize in municipal equipment. While the initial purchase price for a used unit would be lower, we found that most available trucks in our region are of a similar age and condition to our current Unit #23 or only marginally newer. In many cases, the frames and bodies showed significant corrosion, and the equipment packages did not match our standard specifications, which would complicate maintenance and operator training.

   A refurbished unit could potentially extend the life of the fleet at a reduced cost, but we would inherit an unknown maintenance history and likely face higher downtime risk. Our experience with used heavy equipment has been mixed: while some purchases have worked out well, others have resulted in unplanned major repairs within the first 1–3 years of service, which eroded the anticipated savings. Given the critical role that plow trucks play in maintaining safe winter travel, staff do not recommend relying on an older or heavily used unit for front-line operations.

3. Reduce the Number of Plow Routes and Operate with Fewer Trucks:
   Another theoretical option would be to consolidate plow routes and operate with one fewer front-line truck, essentially reassigning the coverage area of Unit #23 to the remaining fleet. While this could avoid the capital cost of a new truck, it would result in longer plowing cycles and reduced service levels during snow events. Based on our route modeling, eliminating one route would add approximately 45–60 minutes to the average plow time for the affected quadrants. During heavy snowfalls or back-to-back storms, this would likely mean that some residential streets, alleys, and cul-de-sacs would not be cleared until well after morning or evening commute times.

   Reduced plowing capacity also has implications for emergency response. Brainerd Fire and Brainerd Police have both expressed concern about any scenario that delays clearing of primary and secondary routes, especially on hills and near critical facilities such as the hospital, schools, and assisted living facilities. Slower response times could increase the risk of accidents, slips and falls, and delays in medical or emergency services.

Impacts on Others:

- Residents and Businesses: A reliable, modern plow fleet directly affects how quickly we can clear streets after snow and ice events. The replacement of Unit #23 will help maintain our current level of service, which targets having all primary routes cleared within 6–8 hours of the end of a typical storm and residential routes within 12–16 hours. Consistent and timely snow removal improves safety for residents commuting to work and school, helps local businesses remain accessible, and reduces the risk of vehicles getting stuck on unplowed streets.

- City Staff and Equipment Operators: Operators have reported that Unit #23 is increasingly uncomfortable and difficult to operate during long shifts due to outdated seating, limited visibility, and finicky controls. The new truck will include upgraded ergonomic seating, better cab insulation, improved LED lighting, and modern spreader and plow controls that reduce operator fatigue. This is particularly important during overnight plowing operations when staff are working long hours in challenging conditions. A more reliable truck also means fewer call-ins for emergency repairs and less stress on our small fleet maintenance team.

- Environmental Considerations: The proposed truck will be equipped with a more efficient engine that meets current emissions standards, as well as a calibrated spreader and pre-wet system that allow for more precise application of salt and brine. Over time, this should reduce the total amount of salt we apply to city streets, which has positive downstream effects on local waterways and roadside vegetation. We will also be standardizing the control systems across more of our fleet, which supports better data collection on material usage and route efficiency.

Additional Comments:

As someone who has lived in Brainerd for most of my life and has worked for the City for over 15 years, I have personally seen how critical it is to maintain a reliable plow fleet. During the March 2019 blizzard, for example, we had multiple trucks down at various times, and it took nearly two full days to get residential streets back to acceptable conditions. Unit #23 played a key role in that event but also suffered a major hydraulic failure that required a costly repair. The pattern over the last several winters has been that every time we push through a big storm, we end up with another significant issue on this truck.

I have also spoken informally with several residents in the northeast part of town who have noticed when their street is plowed later than usual and have asked if we are short on trucks or staff. While we do our best to adjust routes and shifts to cover for equipment downtime, there is a limit to how much we can do without compromising safety and staff well-being. Replacing Unit #23 now, in line with our adopted CIP, is a proactive step that will help us avoid a situation where we are forced into an emergency purchase after a catastrophic failure.

Finally, we anticipate that the auction of Unit #23, even in its current condition, will generate some revenue that can be returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund. Based on recent sales of similar trucks through the state surplus system, we estimate a sale price in the range of $12,000–$18,000, depending on market conditions at the time of auction.

answers.value: Motion to authorize the purchase of one (1) new tandem-axle snowplow truck for the Street Maintenance Division from Northland Truck & Equipment of Duluth, Minnesota, in the total amount of $278,450.00, including chassis, plow and spreader package, and all specified accessories, pursuant to the State of Minnesota cooperative purchasing contract; to adopt the attached resolution approving the purchase and authorizing the Mayor and City Administrator to execute any necessary documents; and to direct staff to dispose of existing Unit #23 (2007 International 7600 plow truck) through the state surplus auction program, with net sale proceeds to be deposited into the Equipment Replacement Fund.

answers.value:
The total estimated cost for the purchase of the new tandem-axle snowplow truck is $278,450.00. This figure includes the chassis, plow and spreader package, underbody scraper, pre-wet system, lighting, safety equipment, and all associated mounting and installation charges. It also includes an allowance for delivery to Brainerd, Minnesota, and applicable sales tax based on our current tax-exempt status for certain components and services.

Funding for this purchase is included in the City’s adopted 2026–2030 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) under the Public Works – Street Maintenance Fleet Replacement program. Specifically, the project is identified as “PW-STR-26-03: Plow Truck Replacement – Unit #23.” The CIP allocates $285,000.00 in 2026 for this purpose, which provides a small contingency above the quoted price to account for any minor changes in final specifications, fuel surcharges, or unforeseen costs that may arise during the build process.

The primary funding source for this purchase will be the City’s Equipment Replacement Fund, which is supported by annual internal service charges to the Public Works operating budget. Over the past several years, we have been setting aside funds each year based on the projected replacement schedule for major fleet assets. This means that the cost of the new truck has effectively been spread out over the life of the existing unit, rather than creating a sudden, unplanned impact on any single year’s operating budget.

From an operational standpoint, we expect that the new truck will reduce maintenance and repair costs compared to Unit #23. Over the last three fiscal years (2023–2025), we have spent an average of approximately $9,800 per year on parts and outside labor for Unit #23, not including internal mechanic labor. Many of these expenses were related to corrosion, hydraulic leaks, and electrical issues that are typical for equipment of this age in a northern climate. By replacing the truck now, we anticipate a period of significantly lower repair costs and less downtime, particularly during the first 5–7 years of service when the truck is under warranty for major components.

There will be some incremental increases in insurance and registration costs associated with placing a newer, higher-value asset into service, but those impacts are modest and have been factored into our long-range fleet operating budget. Fuel usage is expected to be roughly comparable or slightly improved, as the newer engine and more efficient hydraulic systems should offset the similar size and weight of the truck compared to the unit being replaced.

We also expect to recover a portion of the purchase cost through the sale of the existing Unit #23 via the state surplus auction program. While the exact sale price is uncertain and will depend on market conditions and bidder interest at the time of auction, recent sales of comparable plow trucks suggest a likely range of $12,000–$18,000 in revenue. Whatever amount we receive will be returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund, effectively reducing the net long-term cost of the replacement to the City.

In summary, the financial impact of this request has been anticipated and planned for in the City’s CIP and equipment replacement budgeting process. The purchase can be made without additional levy impact beyond what has already been incorporated into prior years’ budgets, and it should result in lower unplanned repair expenses, reduced downtime, and more predictable long-term fleet costs.
